SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents broad empirical evaluation: large-scale automatically labeled data (Freebase + Wikipedia), reported precision figures for trigger (88.9%) and argument (85.4%) labeling, held-out and manual evaluations, P-R curves/average precision, and comparisons to relevant baselines (DMCNN, supervised EE systems, distant supervision baseline). Related work is reported thorough and limitations (noise from distant supervision, Freebase incompleteness) are acknowledged. However, the summary does not indicate ablation or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., KR/TR scoring, two-stage DMCNNs-MIL) and hyperparameters are not reported, which hinders full reproducibility — hence not the highest score. | B(4): The paper presents substantial experiments: automatic labeling on large-scale Freebase+Wikipedia data, held-out and manual evaluations (e.g., 88.9% trigger and 85.4% argument precision), comparisons to prior distant-supervision and supervised baselines, and a two-stage DMCNNs-MIL system to mitigate noise. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and limitations are discussed (noise from distant supervision and Freebase incompleteness). However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses for key components (KR/TR selection, MIL, model variants) and while code is available, hyperparameters are not reported, reducing reproducibility. Given the strong empirical evaluation but these missing analyses/details, the paper merits a solid score of 4.

Related work is reported thorough and limitations (noise from distant supervision, Freebase incompleteness) are acknowledged. However, the summary does not indicate ablation or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., KR/TR scoring, two-stage DMCNNs-MIL) and hyperparameters are not reported, which hinders full reproducibility — hence not the highest score.

Related work coverage is reported as thorough and limitations are discussed (noise from distant supervision and Freebase incompleteness). However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses for key components (KR/TR selection, MIL, model variants) and while code is available, hyperparameters are not reported, reducing reproducibility. Given the strong empirical evaluation but these missing analyses/details, the paper merits a solid score of 4.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses large-scale event extraction using distant supervision, combining Freebase and FrameNet and training neural models (DMCNNs-MIL) on Wikipedia — a topic squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates against standard baselines (e.g., Chen et al. 2015, Mintz et al. 2009) and uses appropriate datasets (Freebase CVTs, FrameNet, Wikipedia, ACE) and metrics (precision/recall, PR curves), matching community expectations. The formulation, methods, and positioning (including code release and comparison to supervised and distant-supervision baselines) make it highly relevant to the venue's audience. | B(5): This work addresses large-scale event extraction with distant supervision—a core NLP problem—using neural models (DMCNNs with MIL) and knowledge resources (Freebase, FrameNet), which aligns well with top NLP/ML venues. The paper uses community-standard datasets (Freebase, Wikipedia, ACE), relevant baselines (Mintz et al., Chen et al.), and evaluation metrics (precision, PR curves, held-out and manual evaluations), indicating appropriate methods and evaluation standards. The contribution is clearly aimed at the NLP/ML research audience and is positioned with thorough related work coverage and released code, making it a strong fit for a top conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow (methodology, experiments, conclusions) and states its main contributions early (automatic labeling using Freebase+FrameNet and a DMCNNs-MIL pipeline). Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as adequate, with informative evaluation metrics and empirical claims (precision/recall figures) reported. Minor presentation issues remain—notation/hyperparameter reporting is incomplete (hyperparameters not reported) and some details about figures/tables/examples are not explicitly mentioned—so clarity is good but could be improved. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and states its main contributions early (automatic labeling approach combining Freebase and FrameNet, KR/TR scoring, and DMCNNs-MIL). Related work coverage is thorough and overall writing quality is described as adequate, so the presentation is generally clear. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and it is unclear from the summary whether notation, figures, and tables are fully detailed, so small clarifications and added experimental details would improve clarity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of scalable event extraction by automatically labeling large-scale training data using Freebase CVTs and FrameNet and demonstrates high precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments) on Wikipedia-derived data. Its combination of world and linguistic knowledge to select key arguments/trigger verbs and the DMCNNs-MIL two-stage model to mitigate distant supervision noise is likely to change how practitioners build training corpora and can be combined with human labels to improve models. Given the large datasets used (~3.8M Freebase instances, ~6.3M Wikipedia articles) and competitive results versus human-labeled data, the work is likely to be widely built upon and opens substantial follow-on work in distantly supervised event extraction, though it is bounded by the usual distant supervision noise and KB incompleteness. | B(4): The paper tackles the important bottleneck of scarce labeled data for event extraction by automatically generating large-scale training data from Freebase CVTs and FrameNet mapped to Wikipedia (3.8M CVT instances, 6.3M articles), achieving high precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments) and competitiveness with human labels. Its two-stage DMCNNs with Multi-Instance Learning and proposed Key Rate / Trigger Rate selection methods meaningfully advance distant supervision for the more complex task of events (not just relations), so researchers are likely to adopt and extend these techniques. While the approach has known limitations (distant-supervision noise and Freebase incompleteness), the strong empirical results and public code make the work influential and broadly useful beyond this subfield.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of scalable event extraction by automatically labeling large-scale training data using Freebase CVTs and FrameNet and demonstrates high precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments) on Wikipedia-derived data. Its combination of world and linguistic knowledge to select key arguments/trigger verbs and the DMCNNs-MIL two-stage model to mitigate distant supervision noise is likely to change how practitioners build training corpora and can be combined with human labels to improve models. Given the large datasets used (~3.8M Freebase instances, ~6.3M Wikipedia articles) and competitive results versus human-labeled data, the work is likely to be widely built upon and opens substantial follow-on work in distantly supervised event extraction, though it is bounded by the usual distant supervision noise and KB incompleteness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important bottleneck of scarce labeled data for event extraction by automatically generating large-scale training data from Freebase CVTs and FrameNet mapped to Wikipedia (3.8M CVT instances, 6.3M articles), achieving high precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments) and competitiveness with human labels. Its two-stage DMCNNs with Multi-Instance Learning and proposed Key Rate / Trigger Rate selection methods meaningfully advance distant supervision for the more complex task of events (not just relations), so researchers are likely to adopt and extend these techniques. While the approach has known limitations (distant-supervision noise and Freebase incompleteness), the strong empirical results and public code make the work influential and broadly useful beyond this subfield.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel angle by combining world knowledge (Freebase CVTs) and linguistic knowledge (FrameNet) to automatically label large-scale event extraction data—extending distant supervision to the more complex event extraction setting. It also proposes new scoring measures (Key Rate from Role Saliency/Event Relevance and Trigger Rate from Trigger Candidate/Type frequencies) and applies a two-stage DMCNNs with Multi-Instance Learning pipeline to mitigate distant-supervision noise. While the neural and MIL components reuse existing techniques (e.g., DMCNNs, MIL), the data-labeling strategy and explicit trigger/argument scoring differentiate the work from prior relation-extraction and supervised EE systems, making it a clear, non-trivial contribution rather than purely incremental. | B(4): The paper presents a notable novel combination of world knowledge (Freebase CVTs) and linguistic knowledge (FrameNet) to automatically label large-scale event extraction data, including new scoring heuristics (Key Rate and Trigger Rate) to select key arguments and triggers — an approach not present in cited prior distant-supervision or supervised EE work. While the use of DMCNNs and multi-instance learning builds on existing architectures (e.g., Chen et al. 2015) and applies known noise-mitigation techniques, integrating these with the automatic labeling pipeline for event extraction is a non-trivial extension that clearly differentiates the contribution from prior relation-extraction distant supervision and supervised EE systems. Overall the contribution is a notable novelty rather than a purely incremental or entirely groundbreaking idea.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel angle by combining world knowledge (Freebase CVTs) and linguistic knowledge (FrameNet) to automatically label large-scale event extraction data—extending distant supervision to the more complex event extraction setting. It also proposes new scoring measures (Key Rate from Role Saliency/Event Relevance and Trigger Rate from Trigger Candidate/Type frequencies) and applies a two-stage DMCNNs with Multi-Instance Learning pipeline to mitigate distant-supervision noise. While the neural and MIL components reuse existing techniques (e.g., DMCNNs, MIL), the data-labeling strategy and explicit trigger/argument scoring differentiate the work from prior relation-extraction and supervised EE systems, making it a clear, non-trivial contribution rather than purely incremental.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable novel combination of world knowledge (Freebase CVTs) and linguistic knowledge (FrameNet) to automatically label large-scale event extraction data, including new scoring heuristics (Key Rate and Trigger Rate) to select key arguments and triggers — an approach not present in cited prior distant-supervision or supervised EE work. While the use of DMCNNs and multi-instance learning builds on existing architectures (e.g., Chen et al. 2015) and applies known noise-mitigation techniques, integrating these with the automatic labeling pipeline for event extraction is a non-trivial extension that clearly differentiates the contribution from prior relation-extraction distant supervision and supervised EE systems. Overall the contribution is a notable novelty rather than a purely incremental or entirely groundbreaking idea.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: the paper reports high precision for auto-labeled data (88.9% trigger, 85.4% argument), compares against relevant baselines (DMCNN, Mintz), and evaluates on held-out and manual (ACE) tests showing the two-stage DMCNNs-MIL reduces distant supervision noise. Mathematical aspects are minimal and there are no derivations to check. However, reproducibility details are incomplete (hyperparameters not reported) and no mention of statistical significance testing is given; the authors also note Freebase’s incompleteness causes false negatives in held-out evaluation. These are minor issues that do not overturn the presented experimental support for the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are supported by empirical evidence: large-scale automatic labeling using Freebase+FrameNet yields high reported precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments), the generated data is evaluated with held-out and manual evaluations, and comparisons to baselines (e.g., DMCNN, Mintz-style DS, supervised EE systems) are reported. Mathematical components are minimal/implementation-focused and appear consistent with described methods (two-stage DMCNNs with MIL); no formal proofs were required. However, reproducibility and rigor have minor shortcomings: hyperparameters are not reported, statistical significance testing is not mentioned, and the authors note Freebase incompleteness induces false negatives in evaluation. Overall, the results justify the conclusions but these methodological gaps prevent a perfect technical score.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: the paper reports high precision for auto-labeled data (88.9% trigger, 85.4% argument), compares against relevant baselines (DMCNN, Mintz), and evaluates on held-out and manual (ACE) tests showing the two-stage DMCNNs-MIL reduces distant supervision noise. Mathematical aspects are minimal and there are no derivations to check. However, reproducibility details are incomplete (hyperparameters not reported) and no mention of statistical significance testing is given; the authors also note Freebase’s incompleteness causes false negatives in held-out evaluation. These are minor issues that do not overturn the presented experimental support for the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by empirical evidence: large-scale automatic labeling using Freebase+FrameNet yields high reported precision (88.9% triggers, 85.4% arguments), the generated data is evaluated with held-out and manual evaluations, and comparisons to baselines (e.g., DMCNN, Mintz-style DS, supervised EE systems) are reported. Mathematical components are minimal/implementation-focused and appear consistent with described methods (two-stage DMCNNs with MIL); no formal proofs were required. However, reproducibility and rigor have minor shortcomings: hyperparameters are not reported, statistical significance testing is not mentioned, and the authors note Freebase incompleteness induces false negatives in evaluation. Overall, the results justify the conclusions but these methodological gaps prevent a perfect technical score.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Chen et al., 2015 DMCNN; Nguyen et al., 2016 supervised EE; Mintz et al., 2009 distant supervision) and evaluates with standard metrics (precision, recall, P-R curves, average precision) on held-out and manual evaluations using ACE and Wikipedia-derived data. Comparisons appear broadly fair and relevant, and related work coverage is described as thorough, but the summary lacks explicit detail on whether all baselines were run under identical data/split and hyperparameter conditions and does not state when prior methods match or outperform the proposed approach. These omissions suggest solid but not fully comprehensive/finely controlled comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Chen et al., 2015 DMCNN; Nguyen et al., 2016 supervised EE; and a Mintz et al., 2009 distant supervision baseline) and evaluates on held-out and manual tests including ACE, which are relevant evaluation settings. Comparisons appear mostly fair, but the summary notes issues that could affect fairness (Freebase incompleteness causing false negatives in held-out evaluation and hyperparameters not reported), so there are minor transparency/condition concerns. The authors discuss limitations of distant supervision, analyze sources of noise, and provide thorough related-work coverage, indicating they acknowledge and analyze differences with prior methods. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only small omissions in reporting/clarity.

Comparisons appear broadly fair and relevant, and related work coverage is described as thorough, but the summary lacks explicit detail on whether all baselines were run under identical data/split and hyperparameter conditions and does not state when prior methods match or outperform the proposed approach. These omissions suggest solid but not fully comprehensive/finely controlled comparisons.

The authors discuss limitations of distant supervision, analyze sources of noise, and provide thorough related-work coverage, indicating they acknowledge and analyze differences with prior methods. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only small omissions in reporting/clarity.

main_claims: A method to automatically label large-scale event extraction training data by combining world knowledge (Freebase) and linguistic knowledge (FrameNet) to detect key arguments and trigger words and label sentences in Wikipedia.

The automatically generated labeled data achieves high precision (88.9% for trigger labeling and 85.4% for argument labeling) and is competitive with human-labeled data; it can be combined with human-labeled data to improve model performance.

A two-stage event extraction approach using Dynamic Multi-pooling CNNs with Multi-Instance Learning (DMCNNs-MIL) mitigates noise in distant supervision and achieves strong performance in held-out and manual evaluations.

Proposed scoring methods: Key Rate (KR) computed from Role Saliency and Event Relevance for selecting key arguments, and Trigger Rate (TR) computed from Trigger Candidate Frequency and Trigger Event Type Frequency for selecting trigger verbs.
_summary.methodology: Use Freebase CVTs as event templates and FrameNet LUs to map lexical triggers; compute Key Rate (KR) to select key arguments per event type and Trigger Rate (TR) to select trigger verbs from Wikipedia-labeled sentences, then train two-stage DMCNNs with Multi-Instance Learning for event classification and argument classification.
datasets: Freebase (snapshot April 23, 2015; filtered to 21 CVT types, ~3.8M instances)
ACE corpus (used for evaluation; mentions ACE 2005 and ACE splits)
baselines: Previous supervised EE systems (e.g., Nguyen et al., 2016)
Distant supervision baseline approach (Mintz et al., 2009)
metrics: Average precision for trigger and argument labeling
limitations: Automatically labeled data contain noise inherent to distant supervision.
Incomplete nature of Freebase causes false negatives in held-out evaluation.
Distant supervision approaches for relation extraction cannot be directly applied to event extraction because events are more complex.
